name: Order Statistics
tagline: Distributions of the max and min of n i.i.d. random variables. CDF of max = F^n; CDF of min = 1 - (1 - F)^n. Cleanest with the uniform distribution.

blocks.text: For n i.i.d. random variables X_1, ..., X_n with CDF F and PDF f, the MAX Z_n has CDF F_Z(x) = F(x)^n (all n must be <= x) and PDF f_Z(x) = n * f(x) * F(x)^(n-1). The MIN Y_n has CDF 1 - (1 - F(x))^n (one minus 'all > x') and PDF f_Y(x) = n * f(x) * (1 - F(x))^(n-1).

For Uniform[0, 1]: E[max of n] = n/(n+1), E[min of n] = 1/(n+1). The min and max of n uniforms have nontrivial covariance: Cov(min, max) = 1/(2(n+1)^2(n+2)) for general n; for n = 2 it equals 1/36, giving correlation 1/2. Order statistics show up in 'first arrival' problems, ant-on-string problems, and any problem about the extreme of multiple samples.

blocks.label: Mental model
blocks.text: Max means 'all are <= x'. Min means 'all are > x'. Both transform to powers of F or 1-F, which differentiate cleanly. Once you have the CDF / PDF of the order statistic, expectation and variance follow standard integration.

whenToUse: Maximum or minimum of n i.i.d. samples.
Time until the first / last of n independent events.
'Random ants on a string' and similar last-arrival problems.
Joint distribution of order statistics (e.g., min and max).
recipe: Find the parent CDF F and PDF f.
For the max: F_max(x) = F(x)^n; differentiate to get f_max.
For the min: F_min(x) = 1 - (1 - F(x))^n; differentiate.
Integrate x * f(x) over the support to get expectation.
For joint statistics (min and max simultaneously), use the joint CDF P(min > a AND max < b) = P(all in (a, b))^n = (F(b) - F(a))^n.
commonPitfalls: Confusing 'max' and 'min' formulas (one is F^n, the other is 1 - (1-F)^n).
Forgetting the factor of n in the PDF (it comes from the chain rule).
Treating min and max as independent -- they have positive correlation.
Using the order-statistic formula on non-iid samples (need same distribution AND independence).
In ant-style problems: forgetting that 'collisions reverse direction' is equivalent to 'ants pass through' due to indistinguishability.

appearsIn.title: Expected Value of Max and Min
